questions.q: Citizenship is dealt with in which Part of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: Part II of the Constitution, covering Articles 5 to 11, deals with citizenship.

questions.q: Which Act of Parliament chiefly governs the acquisition and loss of Indian citizenship?
options: The Representation of the People Act, 1951
questions.explanation: The Citizenship Act of 1955 lays down the detailed law on Indian citizenship.

questions.q: India provides for what kind of citizenship for the whole country?
questions.explanation: Unlike some federations, India has a single citizenship for the entire country.

questions.q: Article 5 of the Constitution determined citizenship as on which date?
options: The commencement of the Constitution, 26 January 1950
questions.explanation: Article 5 fixed citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution on 26 January 1950.

questions.q: Which Ministry of the Government of India principally administers citizenship matters?
options: The Ministry of Law and Justice
questions.explanation: The Ministry of Home Affairs administers matters relating to Indian citizenship.

questions.q: Under the Citizenship Act, which of the following is a recognised mode of acquiring citizenship?
questions.explanation: The Citizenship Act provides for acquisition by birth, descent, registration, naturalisation and incorporation of territory.

questions.q: A person who acquires citizenship of another country generally faces what consequence under Indian law?
questions.explanation: India does not allow full dual citizenship; acquiring another nationality leads to loss of Indian citizenship.

questions.q: Which authority has the power to make laws regarding citizenship under Article 11?
questions.explanation: Article 11 empowers Parliament to regulate the right of citizenship by law.

questions.q: Which provision describes the partial substitute for dual citizenship introduced for persons of Indian origin abroad?
questions.explanation: The Overseas Citizenship of India scheme offers certain rights to persons of Indian origin without full citizenship.

questions.q: Citizenship by descent applies to a person born outside India whose parent is:
options: An Indian citizen at the time of birth
An overseas resident of any country
questions.explanation: Citizenship by descent applies where a parent is an Indian citizen at the time of the person's birth, subject to conditions.

questions.q: The modes of losing Indian citizenship under the Citizenship Act include renunciation, termination and:
questions.explanation: Citizenship may be lost by renunciation, termination, or deprivation by the government in specified cases.

questions.q: Which articles of the Constitution made provisions for migrants to and from Pakistan at the time of Partition?
questions.explanation: Articles 6 and 7 dealt with citizenship rights of persons who migrated from and to Pakistan around Partition.

questions.q: The Citizenship (Amendment) Act that came into effect in 1987 changed citizenship by birth so that, from 1 July 1987, at least one parent must be:
questions.explanation: From 1 July 1987, citizenship by birth required at least one parent to be an Indian citizen.

questions.q: Persons born in India on or after 26 January 1950 but before 1 July 1987 acquired citizenship by birth on what basis?
options: Only if both parents were citizens
By birth in India, irrespective of parents' nationality
questions.explanation: Those born in India in that period were citizens by birth regardless of their parents' nationality.

questions.q: An applicant for citizenship by naturalisation must, among other conditions, ordinarily satisfy a requirement of:
options: Residence in India for a prescribed period
questions.explanation: Naturalisation requires a qualifying period of residence in India along with other prescribed conditions.

questions.q: Which of the following is a right generally reserved for Indian citizens and not foreigners?
options: The right to a fair trial
The right to vote in general elections
questions.explanation: Certain rights, such as voting and contesting elections, are reserved for citizens, while others extend to all persons.

questions.q: Which Fundamental Right is available only to citizens and not to foreigners?
options: The right to freedom of speech under Article 19
questions.explanation: Article 19 freedoms are available only to citizens, while Articles 14 and 21 extend to all persons.

questions.q: Eligibility to be elected President of India requires the candidate to be:
questions.explanation: Only a citizen of India who meets other qualifications can be elected President.

questions.q: Citizenship by registration is generally available to which category of persons?
options: Persons of Indian origin meeting prescribed conditions
questions.explanation: Registration is open to certain categories, such as persons of Indian origin, who fulfil statutory conditions.

questions.q: The Citizenship (Amendment) Act of 2003 introduced the concept of which register related to citizens?
options: The National Register of Indian Citizens
questions.explanation: The 2003 amendment provided for compulsory registration of citizens and a National Register of Indian Citizens.

questions.q: How many citizenships can a person ordinarily hold under Indian law?
questions.explanation: India follows single citizenship and does not generally permit holding full citizenship of two countries.

questions.q: Which document is commonly issued to evidence Indian citizenship for international travel?
questions.explanation: A passport, issued under the Passport Act, is a common document evidencing Indian citizenship for travel.

questions.q: Acquisition of citizenship by 'incorporation of territory' refers to which situation?
options: When a citizen is born abroad
When a foreigner marries a citizen
When a new territory becomes part of India
When a person renounces foreign citizenship
questions.explanation: If a territory becomes part of India, the government may specify the persons of that territory who become citizens.

questions.q: Indian citizenship law is administered under powers traceable to which legislative competence?
questions.explanation: Citizenship and naturalisation are Union List subjects on which only Parliament can legislate.

questions.q: An Overseas Citizen of India cardholder is notably NOT entitled to which of the following?
options: The right to vote in Indian elections
Exemption from registering with police for long stays
Parity with non-resident Indians in some matters
questions.explanation: OCI cardholders enjoy several privileges but are not granted voting rights or eligibility for public office.

questions.q: Which of these is a duty associated with citizenship as set out in the Constitution?
options: To respect the national flag and anthem
To serve in the diplomatic corps
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Duties in Part IV-A include respecting the Constitution, the national flag and the anthem.

questions.q: Termination of citizenship occurs automatically when a citizen voluntarily acquires the citizenship of:
questions.explanation: Under the Citizenship Act, voluntarily acquiring another country's citizenship terminates Indian citizenship.

questions.q: The earliest Constitution provisions on citizenship, Articles 5 to 11, were intended mainly to:
options: Provide a permanent code of citizenship law
Identify citizens at the commencement of the Constitution
questions.explanation: Articles 5 to 11 were transitional provisions identifying citizens as on 26 January 1950, leaving the rest to Parliament.

questions.q: A person who renounces Indian citizenship makes a declaration that takes effect upon being:
questions.explanation: A declaration of renunciation takes effect when it is registered by the prescribed authority.

questions.q: Citizenship by descent for a person born outside India after a certain date generally requires the birth to be:
options: Registered at an Indian consulate within the prescribed period
questions.explanation: For citizenship by descent in recent periods, the birth must be registered at an Indian consulate within the prescribed time.
